items.title: Super Bowl 60 recap: Rewatch the ads after Seahawks clinch the championship
items.description: The Seattle Seahawks defeated the New England Patriots in Super Bowl 60. CNBC covered all the top ads and business news from football's biggest night.

items.title: Consumer Staples Showdown: Is FSTA or RSPS the Better Buy Right Now?
items.author: Katie Brockman
items.description: The article compares two consumer staples ETFs: Fidelity's FSTA and Invesco's RSPS. FSTA offers a significantly lower expense ratio (0.08% vs 0.40%), larger assets under management ($1.3B vs $232M), and better 1-year and 5-year returns. However, FSTA is concentrated in mega-cap holdings like Costco, Walmart, and P&G (37% of assets), while RSPS uses equal-weighting across 36 stocks, potentially reducing volatility. The choice depends on investor preferences: FSTA for potential outperformance with larger holdings, or RSPS for more balanced risk distribution.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: FSTA demonstrates superior performance with lower expense ratio (0.08%), higher 1-year return (8.34%), better 5-year growth ($1,385 vs $1,067), larger AUM ($1.3B), and lower maximum drawdown (-16.57%). These metrics indicate better value and risk-adjusted returns for investors.

Costco is highlighted as a top holding in FSTA, representing a significant portion of the fund's assets. Its inclusion as a major position suggests confidence in its performance as a defensive consumer staples stock.

Walmart is identified as one of FSTA's top three holdings, accounting for nearly 37% of assets combined with Costco and P&G. Its prominence indicates strong positioning in the consumer staples sector.
P&G is listed as one of FSTA's top three concentrated holdings, representing a significant portion of the fund's assets and reflecting its importance in the consumer staples sector.
